claim.hypotheses_location: Study 1 section: Hypotheses (Hypotheses 1–3b) following the 'Study 1' heading; Study 2 section: Hypotheses (Hypotheses 4–5) following the 'Study 2' heading.

data.source: Laboratory experiments using step-level and assurance public goods dilemmas
data.sample_size: Study 1: 72 recruited (final N=62 after exclusions); Study 2: 84 recruited (final N=65 after exclusions)
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual decision in each game (Study 1: contribution amount per game; Study 2: binary cooperation choice per game)
data.notes: Within-subjects designs. Study 1: each participant made 12 independent decisions across 3 threshold levels (low/medium/high) and 4 outcome variance conditions; endowment 125 points, paid at 1 point = $0.01 for one randomly selected decision. Study 2: each participant made 6 assurance-game decisions across 3 outcome variance conditions and 2 group sizes; contribution all-or-nothing (125 points). Some participants were excluded for incomplete data or misunderstanding instructions. Orders were blocked/rotated to mitigate order effects. Outcomes presented as uniform distributions with equal likelihood.

method.description: Two laboratory experiments examined how outcome variance in the value of a public good affects contributions. Study 1 used a step-level public goods dilemma with varying provision thresholds and outcome variance conditions, collecting continuous contribution amounts and stated strategy reasons. Study 2 used an assurance public goods dilemma with binary cooperation choices, manipulating outcome variance and group size, and collected strategy reasons.
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants and provide instructions and comprehension quizzes.
Present a series of independent public goods games with specified endowments, thresholds (Study 1), outcome variance conditions (both studies), and group size (Study 2).
In Study 1, allow participants to allocate any portion of 125 points to personal vs. shared account across 12 games (3 thresholds × 4 variance levels).
In Study 2, have participants make all-or-nothing (125 points) contribution decisions across 6 games (3 variance levels × 2 group sizes).

Randomly combine each participant’s decisions with others post-session to determine whether thresholds are met (Study 1) or unanimous cooperation occurs (Study 2), then compute payouts for one randomly selected decision.
Collect self-reported strategy/motivation for each decision and manipulation check items.
Exclude participants with incomplete data or evidence of misunderstanding.

Analyze Study 1 contributions with repeated-measures ANOVA (controlling for threshold), and analyze Study 2 cooperation with Generalized Estimating Equations (logistic). Conduct chi-square tests on strategy choice proportions.
method.models: Study 1: Repeated-measures ANOVA; chi-square tests on proportions for strategy choices. Study 2: Generalized Estimating Equations (logistic regression; Wald chi-square tests); chi-square tests on proportions for strategy choices.
method.outcome_variable: Study 1: Contribution amount to the shared account (0–125 points). Study 2: Cooperation (1 if contributing 125 points to shared account; 0 otherwise).
method.independent_variables: Outcome variance/prospects (no variance; gains-only; losses-and-gains). Study 1 additionally manipulated provision threshold (low, medium, high). Study 2 additionally manipulated group size (2 vs 8).
method.control_variables: Study 1 analyses reported contributions controlled for threshold level; order of games was counterbalanced within threshold blocks.

results.summary: Across two experiments, outcome variance reduced contributions only when the distribution of possible outcomes included loss prospects. When prospects entailed gains only, contributions/cooperation did not differ from no-variance conditions. In Study 1 (step-level dilemma), contributions were significantly lower in losses-and-gains conditions than in no-variance and gains-only conditions; strategy reports indicated increased loss aversion and fear of others’ non-contribution under loss prospects. In Study 2 (assurance dilemma), cooperation was significantly lower with losses-and-gains prospects; group size reduced cooperation (small > large), but did not significantly moderate the loss-prospect effect. Participants reported greater concern about incurring a loss in losses-and-gains conditions in both studies.

numerical_results.effect_size: Wald χ2(1) = 19.74 (vs pooled no-variance and gains-only, 0.74)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Study 2: Group size effect on cooperation (small vs large)

metadata.original_paper_title: Is it even worth it? The effect of loss prospects in the outcome distribution of a public goods dilemma
